7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You know the guy. Maybe you’ve worked with him. Headphones permanently on, twelve terminal windows, communicates in grunts and pull requests, ships more code before lunch than the rest of the team does all week. The 10x engineer. Silicon Valley’s favorite cryptid.
Here’s my claim, and I’ll spend this whole article defending it and then honestly attacking it: that person’s superpower is depreciating. Not because they got worse, but because the thing they were 10x at, personally typing correct code very fast, is exactly the thing that stopped being scarce. The new outlier isn’t the person who writes the most code. It’s the person who can keep five parallel streams of machine generated work moving without shipping garbage.
Different skill. Often a different person. And that second part is what nobody wants to talk about.
Where the 10x myth even came from
Quick history, because the number “10x” gets thrown around like it fell from the sky. It traces back to a 1968 study by Sackman, Erikson and Grant, which found enormous productivity differences between individual programmers, in some measurements more than tenfold. The study was small and the methodology has been argued about for fifty years, but the observation stuck because everyone who’s…
